quoteLast: of October 5th (a beautiful day), I had a superb view of the vast panorama to the north and west.  To the southwest, about Dallas, could be seen the smoke of camp-fires, indicating the presence of a large force of the enemy, and the whole line of railroad from Big Shanty up to Allatoona (full fifteen miles) was marked by the fires of the burning railroad.
title: Memoirs of General W. T. Sherman, Volume II., Part 3
author: Sherman, William T. (William Tecumseh), 1820-1891

quoteFirst: In this instance, as well as in every other which occured in the course of these transactions, Gates behaved with such attention and humanity, to all whom the fortune of war threw into his hands, as does honor to his character. The badness of the roads, and the starving condition of the cattle for want of forage, together with one incessent rain, like a continued thunder shower from
quoteLast: of the 9th till long after the day closed, and other difficulties, prevented the army’s reaching Saratoga though no more than about six miles distant, before night, and then worn down with excessive fatigue. During the rain a body of militia continued their march, and got in above Gates’ army, but some way below Fort Edward.
title: The history of the rise, progress, and establishment of the independence of the United States of America, Vol. 2 (of 3)
author: Gordon, William, 1728-1807

quoteFirst: I turned in, and--what will not youth and fatigue do?--I fell once more fast asleep, and never opened my eyes until Obed shook me in my cot
quoteTime: about eight o’clock in the morning
title: Tom Cringle's Log
author: Scott, Michael, 1789-1835

quoteTime: about eight o'clock in the morning
quoteLast: when my boat reached this burning ghat. Already one body had been placed on a funeral pyre of wood.
title: The Critic in the Orient
author: Fitch, George Hamlin, 1852-1925

quoteTime: About eight o'clock in the morning
quoteLast: we went a little from the coast to reconnoitre some Moors who had shewn themselves. We found two or three wretched tents, in which there were some Mooresses almost all naked, they were as ugly and frightful as the sands they inhabit.
title:
Narrative of a Voyage to Senegal in 1816
Undertaken by Order of the French Government, Comprising an Account of the Shipwreck of the Medusa, the Sufferings of the Crew, and the Various Occurrences on Board the Raft, in the Desert of Zaara, at St. Louis, and at the Camp of Daccard. to Which Are Subjoined Observations Respecting the Agriculture of the Western Coast of Africa, from Cape Blanco to the Mouth of the Gambia.
author: Savigny, Jean Baptiste Henri, 1793-1843; Corréard, Alexandre, 1788-1857

quoteLast: the general woke up, and though he was very low and his weakness was extreme, the long sleep which he had enjoyed seemed to have greatly relieved his sufferings. The doctor, after carefully counting his pulse, began removing the bandages which he had placed.
title: The Smuggler Chief: A Novel
author: Aimard, Gustave, 1818-1883; Wraxall, Lascelles, Sir, 1828-1865 [Translator]

quoteFirst: But times there are when sun and man, by stress of work, or clouds, or light, or it may be some Process of the Equinox, make draughts upon the untilted day, and solace themselves in the morning. For lack of dew the sun draws lengthy sucks of cloud quite early, and men who have labored far and dry, and scattered the rime of the night with dust, find themselves ready
quoteLast: for the golden encouragement of gentle ale.
title: Mary Anerley: A Yorkshire Tale
author: Blackmore, R. D. (Richard Doddridge), 1825-1900

quoteLast: , and we weighed anchor. It soon freshened somewhat, and we worked down the coast, tacking on and off. First we passed the point forming the north extremity of Casuarina Bay, distinguished by a single palm tree which rises high above the jungle, and next came abreast of Kópenhéat, marked by a grove of palms and a hut, finally bringing up at 1.15 P.M. in 9 fathoms, at a spot well protected by a reef from the S.W.
title: In the Andamans and Nicobars: The Narrative of a Cruise in the Schooner "Terrapin"
author: Kloss, C. Boden (Cecil Boden), 1877-

quoteFirst: But even then the figure of the old man often started before me, and a sense of uneasiness about George made a strong undercurrent to my drifting dreams. I was awakened at
quoteTime: about eight o'clock in the morning
quoteLast: by the engineer, who told me one of the old man's sons had been picked up and was now on board.
title: Selected Stories of Bret Harte
author: Harte, Bret, 1836-1902
